posts.name: Matthew Benjamin
posts.cooked:
As usual, excellent

robertparker.com

Robert Parker Wine Advocate - Unbiased Wine Reviews

The ultimate guide for exploring the world of fine wines. The Wine Advocate offers valuable insights, practical recommendations and expert knowledge through the fascinating world of wines.

link_counts.title: Robert Parker Wine Advocate - Unbiased Wine Reviews

posts.name: John Maguire
posts.cooked: A great read, thoughtful as usual. Thanks for posting it!

posts.name: Glen Gold
posts.cooked: I learn something new every time. For instance, it turns out these aren’t the little crystals that appear on the bottom of corks. Carry on.

posts.name: Richard Frankel
posts.cooked: Very interesting, and addresses a subject discussed a lot by we wine lovers. And yet, every rule has its exceptions. One of WK’s favorite young producers is Charles Lachaux (of Arnoux Lachaux) who makes huge changes frequently - the opposite of what Kelley describes as a hallmark of a great producer.

posts.name: David Patte, Sun Break Wines
posts.cooked: same : ) would make a great band name too. I like the opinion piece quite a bit, still mulling it over. Each winery I worked for (and as I observe my colleagues for that matter) have had very different practices in many areas (use of whole cluster, oak and élevage, use of sulfites, filtering, etc), but not so much in others (type of fermenter, press, etc). The question of vision is interesting and perhaps harder to me to evaluate because in the end I think we all envision producing great wines of character and complexity (albeit differing expressions such as striving for elegance vs more robust expressions, as just one example). But I see the point of practices not supporting the vision. I love the concept of compounding effects – that is very relevant here because there are so many steps in the grape growing and wine making process.

posts.name: Robert Dentice
posts.cooked: On my last trip to Burgundy I did Charles Lachaux in the morning and Mugnier in the afternoon. It was quite a study in contrasts! We visited William the next day

posts.name: Cody Rasmussen
posts.cooked: Thanks for sharing @M_Benjamin, this is a great read. Love the idea of marginal gains (very Roberto Conterno-esque) and fortune favoring the conservative/consistent

posts.name: Matthew Benjamin
posts.cooked: Yes staying with consistent principles but having holistic approach to the various interactions is key imo

posts.name: Tom R W  \/\/@rren
posts.cooked:
Thanks for posting! Dense, thoughtful read.

My background is biomedical devices & manufacturing, and William’s piece reminds me of some of my conclusions in that sphere.
On the mfg side many are constantly undergoing wholesale change looking for that “silver bullet” technique to catapult them to excellence but are only left with a wildly inconsistent process and similar results. I concluded over the years that a refined process with many thoughtful, incremental gains stacked on top of each other was the best pathway to consistently excellent and repeatable results.

posts.name: Benjamin Weiss
posts.cooked:
“Over the course of this decade, informed by exchanges with some exceptional individuals in other fields, I have instead come to the persuasion that the aggregation of marginal gains is what explains why some producers attain a level of excellence that few others are able to emulate. Quantitative, incremental refinements, in other words, can compound to create a qualitative difference.”

Love this and Williams’s words extend far, far beyond wine. Business, investing, pretty much anything. While the masses always gravitate to get rich quick schemes, the real secret to durable advantage is often a slight edge and patience in a compounding system.

posts.name: William Kelley
posts.cooked:
Thanks for the kind and thoughtful comments, folks!

Writing this piece was very helpful for me to refine my own thinking about the subject.

I wish these insights, such as they are, had come to me at the very beginning of my writing career. But they are in fact more of an extrapolation from observation than a vision constructed from first principles, so it took time for the pieces to fall into place.

posts.name: Marcus Goodfellow
posts.cooked:
Just started the article but since you’re here…mis-directed hard work is vastly worse than laziness.

The lazy winemaker sitting around in the winery might happen to see something occuring in the natural process occuring that, in catching his un-harnessed attention, he can opt to address. The winemaker who is constantly grinding and occupying time on tasks that fail to actually matter in the outcome of the wine has no attention to spend on seeing what would actually make an impact.

posts.name: William Kelley
posts.cooked:
Interestingly, a friend who has been extremely successful in the finance/investing world just wrote me with the opposite perspective, suggesting that misdirected hard work tends to have one “bump into things” in a way that laziness doesn’t.

What both of your observations share is an emphasis on the importance of observation: whether that’s not missing the interesting development in the natural process, or not missing the significance of the facts you “bump into” in the midst of misdirected activity.

posts.name: Robert Dentice
posts.cooked:
William_Kelley:

Interestingly, a friend who has been extremely successful in the finance/investing world just wrote me with the opposite perspective, suggesting that misdirected hard work tends to have one “bump into things” in a way that laziness doesn’t.

Both points are somewhat valid; however, in finance I would rather have an extremely hard worker than a lazy one. Almost every single highly successful in my field is one of if not the hardest workers.

Also the compensation structure fits more with the former model especially if you work at hedge fund. In Finance the risk reward is titled toward taking as much risk as possible.

posts.name: larry schaffer
posts.cooked:
I think the tern ‘laziness’ as it pertains to winemaking is an interesting one. Much of winemaking is ‘passive’ - once the ‘hard work’ of harvest is done and the wines are put to bed, the ‘hard work’ aspect of winemaking is replaced by waiting . . . and waiting. And ‘tinkering’ at this point may or may not yield ‘better results’. Would ‘passive’ be perceived as ‘lazy’ to many? Probably - but it should not be . . .

Too much to unravel at this point but just thought I’d add that ‘tidbit’ in here.

Cheers.

posts.name: Marcus Goodfellow
posts.cooked:
I think you would find in both winemaking and finance that someone working hard all the time on things that don’t matter still isn’t the successful person you’re talkng about.

There isn’t a person that I consider to be among the best winemakers that I know who does not work hard, there are no lazy winemakers that do great work. But there are many very, very diligent people in wine production that don’t recognize or prioritize the best opportunities and the processes that will yield the best results.

posts.name: Nick Christie
posts.cooked:
I enjoyed reading these perspectives today, particularly the addendums from Marcus. Some lovely concepts upon which to ponder. The dialogue evokes the iconic quote from The Art of War:

Strategy without tactics is the slowest route to victory, tactics without strategy is the noise before defeat

Being around William’s work (as well re-reading his ideas in this piece) has been strongly persuasive. What kind of ‘investment’ promotes a superior viticultural achievement? Continued marginal improvements in infrastructure and thoughtful, focused, patient years of consistency & observation in lieu of desperate action for action’s sake, even if that can feel more kinetically vibrant.

And I echo the kind sentiments shared across the two threads: that lesson can improve one’s life & relationships, too.

posts.name: Adam Frisch
posts.cooked:
I certainly agree with the passage that so many winemaking choices are made simply for practical reasons. Can’t over-vintage in barrels at the custom crush facility? Well, then you have to bottle - ready or not. Can’t free up a stainless steel tank for that wine that asks for it, so you have to barrel age it. Etc, etc.

This is - I think - a reason many old world wineries are perhaps more consistent than we are here in the US. They own their cellars, their facilities, their caves. It’s repeatable each year, open to small nuances. Whereas a small US producer has probably bounced around 3 different wineries or facilities by the time the brand even establishes itself. God knows how many different natural yeast strains have kicked off the fermentation in all these places. No caves to serenely sample either for 99.999% of us - just a cold storage facility in some industrial complex hosting the bottled.

posts.name: Marcus Goodfellow
posts.cooked:
Nick_Christie:

Continued marginal improvements in infrastructure and thoughtful, focused, patient years of consistency & observation in lieu of desperate action for action’s sake, even if that can feel more kinetically vibrant.

Just off the cuff as we’re racking the cellar for bottling 2023s in 3 weeks.

There are three VERY powerful actions at work here.

  1. Grapes have been grown for millinia and fermented nearly as long. But each grower only has so many years to do the farming themselves. Feedback for vineyard choices can take years to see in the vineyard and longer to see in the wines.

Having a generational history sounds great, but in reality is harder to wrangle usefulness out of than one might think. Anyone with kids knows what communication with them is like and anyone who was a kid knows what listening to your parents is like.(How many, many generational transitions in Burgundy have been away from chemicals, high yields, etc.) This is where a community certainly helps, and where sharing ideas and ways that work becomes regionally important. But that serves as a base line for quality in the region.

  1. To consistently surpass the norm for quality farming and winemaking requires imagination, vision, strategy, work ethic, and absolutely talent.

Grape growing and winemaking function on an annual cycle, you do not get to work at them every day like painting or cooking. A person can prune more than half their life and not do it more than 35-40 times.

So to stand out above a peer group, one needs to both “get it”, as in have a green thumb which is definitely about understanding how things grow and having an understanding of what will help to bring about the best results in the vineyard. The faster that develops the more opportunities a person has to develop a layered and nuanced approach to growing/fermentation. And those opportunities are definitely limited.

  1. You also need to be an early adopter. Constant experimentation to find out what you don’t know that you don’t know. Whether those experiments ever make it out of the winery or not, you have to be doing them. If you don’t, you lose too many years and are behind the curve with the people who are doing them. Not every hunch is correct, but having good instincts really does matter.

But balance that with understanding fashion is temporary. Terroir is not. I stay away from adjuncts, enzymes, tannin adds, fining, etc because I feel they are usually more about convenience than a unique nature (they can’t be unique as the products are very consistent). Vineyard changes need to be thoughtful so that an experiment isn’t suddenly a catastrophic event and one has to be real in understanding that grape growing and wine production are also about successful management of logistical and labor challenges.

But you just don’t have the time in a single lifetime to work entirely cautiously. Fortune favors the brave. For a generational winery like Chave, maybe the bravery of 6-7 generations ago covered most of the bases but for a vineyard like X-Novo jumping into the deep end of clonal options was a smart move.

Last, I can’t emphasize enough that Grand Cru is Grand Cru. The confluence of latitude, soils, wind, elevation, aspect, etc. defines what can be acheived as much as the farming and winemaking. Behind every great producer is a great vineyard.

posts.name: Mike Grammer
posts.cooked: I have never forgotten a conversation with Byron Kosuge in Sonoma that really moved me. He said something to this effect. Many jobs, you do a process thousands of times. As a winemaker, if you’re lucky, you may have 40 chances (give or take) to get it right in your lifetime. It was sobering for me to think of winemaking in those terms, and I have always been grateful that he shared that perspective.
